findings.title: Homepage title is just the brand
findings.recommendation: Fine for a known brand; consider "Brand — what you do" (~50–60 chars) to also rank for the value proposition.

findings.title: Meta description may be truncated
findings.evidence: The Verge is about technology and how it makes us feel. Founded in 2011, we offer our audience everything from breaking news to reviews to award-winning features and investigations, on our site, in video, and in podcasts.

findings.title: No H1 heading
findings.recommendation: Add a single H1 that states the page's main topic.

findings.title: Links with no anchor text
findings.recommendation: Give links descriptive anchor text for users and crawlers.

findings.title: Content is fairly hard to read
findings.recommendation: Consider shorter sentences/simpler words unless the audience is specialist.

findings.title: Dominant topics detected
findings.detail: verge (16), andrew (15), sony (11), webster (10), game (10), apple (9)  ·  phrases: andrew webster, dominic preston, jay peters

findings.title: No visible date signal
findings.detail: No published/modified date in metadata or near the top.
findings.recommendation: Expose a publish or last-updated date to signal freshness.

findings.title: Few question-based headings
findings.detail: 0 of 9 subheadings are phrased as questions.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite key H2/H3s as the questions users actually ask — AI engines match answers to question-shaped headings. (Most useful on article/guide pages; lower priority here.)

findings.title: No direct answer up top
findings.detail: The opening doesn't state a concise answer/definition.
findings.recommendation: Lead with a 1–2 sentence direct answer before the details; AI overviews lift the top summary.

findings.title: No llms.txt
findings.recommendation: Optionally publish /llms.txt to guide AI crawlers to your key content. Evidence for ranking impact is still thin — treat as low-cost, low-certainty.

findings.title: AI crawlers are blocked in robots.txt
findings.detail: Disallowed from the site root: ChatGPT-User (OpenAI (ChatGPT on-demand fetch)), ClaudeBot (Anthropic (Claude)), Claude-Web (Anthropic (Claude on-demand)), PerplexityBot (Perplexity), Google-Extended (Google (Gemini / AI Overviews training)), CCBot (Common Crawl (feeds many LLMs)), Bytespider (ByteDance).
findings.recommendation: If you want visibility in AI answers, allow these agents. If the block is intentional (content protection), ignore this.
findings.evidence: ChatGPT-User (OpenAI (ChatGPT on-demand fetch)), ClaudeBot (Anthropic (Claude)), Claude-Web (Anthropic (Claude on-demand)), PerplexityBot (Perplexity), Google-Extended (Google (Gemini / AI Overviews training)), CCBot (Common Crawl (feeds many LLMs)), Bytespider (ByteDance)

findings.title: Heavy images (4 over 200 KB)
findings.detail: Largest: 1108 KB. Checked 10 of 85 images (HEAD on the raw src). All carry srcset, so browsers likely fetch smaller variants — this is the fallback weight.
findings.recommendation: Compress/resize and serve modern formats (AVIF/WebP); heavy images are a top LCP cause. Also lighten the src fallback (it's what scrapers and srcset-unaware clients download).

findings.title: Legacy image formats with real weight (8)
findings.detail: JPEG/PNG files over 100 KB that AVIF/WebP would shrink substantially.
findings.recommendation: Serve AVIF/WebP with <picture> or content negotiation.
findings.evidence: 268597_BITTORRENT_25_V200.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0%2C0%2C100%2C100&w=2400 (121 KB, jpeg), 247361_PS5_Pro_VPavic_99.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=16.642156862745%2C0%2C66.71568627451%2C100&w=2400 (125 KB, jpeg), 247361_PS5_Pro_VPavic_99.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0%2C0.03673769287289%2C100%2C99.926524614254&w=2400 (196 KB, jpeg), 268434_Rivian_Factory_ATahlier_0009_3x4.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=12.5%2C0%2C75%2C100&w=2400 (808 KB, jpeg)

findings.title: Images without width/height attributes
findings.recommendation: Add width/height (or CSS aspect-ratio) so the browser reserves space — prevents layout shift (CLS).
